speaker: interviewer
text: It is October 4, 1996. So, Mrs. Rumstein, when we left off, you mentioned that you were able to see the bombing of Dresden from a certain--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --distance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what did you see? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we were in the factory. And instead of bringing us on top of the factory, they brought us first in the basement. And from the basement, the people who worked in the factory went into-- that's called-- what do you call, A [GERMAN], that's where they had the bomb Keller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a cellar?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cellar, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't let us in. They put us out-- put us out right in front of the factory. And as we were out, it was a cold-- it was February 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the-- we saw fire. The sky was, was red. And we saw the, you know, the dogfights and the fights and, and, and the flag, you know. They're shooting at the-- we saw airplanes coming down. That's when I was so afraid. And oh my god, if something wouldn't happen? What is it we are not dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what I'm trying to say-- so it was-- it was-- afterwards, people still are very upset that the British bombed Dresden at that time and that so many people got killed. A lot of Germans got killed. And not knowing when people say that-- meanwhile, they had a camp right close by where people got killed, innocent people. I'm sure there were also innocent people who got killed. But this was our hope that we knew that something is going on, that we were happy to see that there was a fighting going on, that something is going to happen, that, that the war is coming closer. So we were kind of terrible afraid but happy to see that something is happening, that not-- nothing is too far away anymore. And so we were out. And we saw all the-- we saw the-- Dresden burning. The sky was, like, red. And that's-- after that, you know, we were put-- we were-- went back, back to the barracks and back to work. And one day in-- I think it was end of March, they, they gathered us together from the barracks, line up, and got us to cattle wagons again, put us in there. We didn't know where they wanted to bring us. Later, we heard they want us-- to bring us to the main camp, which-- Flossenburg, that was full. Because there, they still-- there, I saw heaps of dead people lying there when we-- when we go-- went through. And we were on the road, on the-- on the train for at least maybe, maybe two or three weeks. They didn't know where to bring us. And what they did in-between, they loosened up the, the-- you know, they opened up the train, disconnected it, and put, put other trains in-between with soldiers and with ammunitions because it was kind of a camouflage. Because the fight, they were-- constantly were planes going, fighting. I remember we had those little things where we got our soups in. It was like metal-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't say that's a plate. But we used to put this on our head. We were hoping, like, this would help us. And so in-between, we were going-- we were going. We went through Czechoslovakia. I remember we stopped in Pilsen. Because we didn't get anything to eat. I remember we got, like-- in that plate, we got a, a, a, a little bit soup of something. I tell you, they didn't know where, where to bring us. We were hoping, since we saw we were in Czechoslovakia, that they might bring us back to Terezin. But they didn't. They brought us to to Mauthausen. And it was March, beginning of April. Again, no food, nothing. They let us out occasionally from the trains to go on the-- underneath the train to have your, your functions. And I remember there were cherry blossom trees-- cherry trees, you know, on the side of the train. And we went-- we ate the cherry blossoms. We ate grass. And whatever water there was, we took and we drank it, whatever, whatever. So we figured, you know, it's good for the animals. It'll be good for-- I mean, good for us. We, we needed to have something. Until we-- all of a sudden, we looked, and we saw Mauthausen. That-- the train went into Mauthausen. Well, we knew about Mauthausen coming from Austria because Mauthausen was one of the oldest concentration camps in Austria. It was a quarry. And a lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] political prisoners were there already long before and a lot of criminals, murderers. It was like a, a, a-- in a prison. And somehow, somehow, we thought, maybe-- oh, we are in Austria. Maybe it won't be so bad. As we lined up-- as we lined up the road there to go into the camp, all the locals were standing left and right, laughing and saying, well, that's a good place for you. There you will-- you will find your eternal rest here. That's what they told us. This was-- these were the Austrians, the good Austrians that-- they say they didn't know-- like, the surrounding people who didn't know that there was a camp. And we went up there, terrible afraid. We were concentrated in one area. And it says, we're going to take a bath. We're going to take a shower. And I started crying. I was like, I mean, everybody-- but I was like-- I was hysterical because I thought, that said, now, go in a shower, and we're going to be gassed. And as I was crying like this, one guy came to me. He was a guard in-- was a guard. He used to be in the army. And I don't know, they sent him back there as a-- as a guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me in German, why do you cry? I said, because we are going to get gassed now. And I'm so afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said in German to me, he says, no, you won't. Don't forget, this was April-- end of April-- end of April. And he said to me, no, you-- you're not going to be gassed. You're not going to be gassed.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, where do you come from? I said, I come from Vienna. He was-- he said he's from Munich. And he says, wait here. And he went. And he says, who, who are you with here? I said, well, my sister and my mother is here. And he brought us something to eat-- I forgot what it is-- which we-- [LAUGHS] which we ate very fast. And he disappeared. He disappeared-- didn't see him until later.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all right, I was a little bit-- I told the other people-- I said, I heard from this guy we are not going to be gassed. We're just really going into the shower. And we got some in the shower. And he gave us-- they gave us some men underwear which, which were full of lice already. But this is what we had. Don't forget, we were on the train for three weeks. We were, like, smell-- smelly and dirty. You cannot imagine. And then, after we stayed there for a while, they took us into the gypsy camp. There is a big quarry there. People is on top. And there's a valley. And a valley-- and they-- stairs going down there, like very, very high stairs, and people used to be-- steep.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the whole thing is very steep. People used to have to carry from downstairs those heavy stones up. They didn't make it? They threw them down there. I mean, this was constantly going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But with us, they just put us down into this gypsy camp. It's called gypsy camp because most of them were gypsies they had down there, gypsies and Jews, of the, the most-- of the underworld, the under people. And there we were, lying sick, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just waiting, whatever happens. And-- but we heard in the distance already for a-- for a while, like, cannons, and-- you know. But we still don't know what it is. We didn't know what it meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was artillery fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Artillery fire, fighting--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --going on, but in a-- in a distance, you know. And we were there. I wouldn't say-- we had no feelings whatsoever, just lie there and wait. Again, they couldn't-- because there were a lot of air raids going on, we didn't get any food again there, nothing. And that went on for, for quite a while. And people were dying. And I could see them. You know, they put them in, like-- in rows, this way, this way, this way, like heaps of dead people there. But our feelings were like-- we were completely numb. And one day in May, I saw a jeep-- a jeep. Some people were saying it was the British. I think it were-- it were the American that came. I'm almost sure. Because later on, I learned there's a lot of-- it was not the Russians. It were the American who liberated us. And again, there was no joy. There was no happiness. It was just-- I can't say resignation because we were completely numb after all this time. And, and that was actually-- this was our liberation. The only thing we did-- we saw that the, the, the gypsies, kind of-- maybe they were stronger. Maybe they were there longer. They went out. And they came back with chickens--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --afterwards, you know. They had chickens-- and with food, and with this. And Gertie and I-- the last strength we had-- we said, look, we are in Austria. Maybe we-- maybe there are farmers around there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went. We knocked at every door. We said, we are Austrians! We are Austrian! Can we have something to eat? So one of these farmers opened the door and let us in. Mind you, it was like a day or two after the, the liberation. And he gave us a glass of wine.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] We were sitting there. And before we even drank it, two guys stormed in with guns. And he saw us sitting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, what are those Jewish bitches doing here-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, Jewish bitches doing here? Out of here! And he held the gun. He didn't shoot us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we started running like crazy. We ran back to the-- to the camp without food. [LAUGHS] Just-- we just laid there, ready to die. And this was the-- this was our liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What What happened after your liberation? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the liberation, like I said, my mother was very sick. And they had some, like, either was it Red Cross cars or something going around, collecting people who were-- you know. So I made sure that my mother should be up. They should take her up to the main camp. Didn't know where they're taking her, but take her up because she was-- she was ready to die. Gertie and I, we were lying there. She was, like, over there. And I was over here. And I couldn't even crawl over there. I couldn't. I said-- I went-- once a day, I visited her. I went back. And I said, I have no strength. And there we were lying there maybe for-- then we got something to eat, which was very bad because whatever we got to eat, we got diarrhea. You know, they gave us whatever the American rations were. It was like beans-- bean soup or something. And that was-- that gave us duress.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one day, we said, how long we are-- may be done? Nobody's-- cares for us. We're just down there. So we decided to go up there to the, the quarry steps there-- that's the only way you could get up-- and look for my mother. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we attempted that. It took us hours. I thought I have a heart attack every time I was [LAUGHS] going up one step. I said, Gertie, I can't. So she hit me. And when she couldn't go on, I hit her. I just, just-- we have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we made it up the stairs. And I went from place to place. And I called, Mama, Mama, Mama? Once I heard her call, Thea! [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I crawled through the window. This was already-- she was in the infirmary there. And that's where we stayed, in the infirmary for another few weeks. Then we were already liberated. And that was the-- this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you left the infirmary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the three of you stayed together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed together, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed together. I-- we had nothing to wear. We, we had lousy underwear. Even the infirmary, there was-- you know, they didn't have all the facilities to take care of the people. We found some tablecloths there. And we made, made us each-- I was-- because I learned how to sew and my mother could sew, we, we cut it up. And we made us each a little dress and a little satchel. And we said, we have to go back someplace. It can't be forever-- it was already-- it was already end of May. It was beginning of June already. I mean, the war ended in-- May 7.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us into trains to go to Vienna. But we didn't get far. We got to the Russian Zone. They made us get out of the trains, sprayed us with DDT, you know. We had to go that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to start walking. Now, my mother couldn't walk anymore. So as we got into this little town in Austria, it was all Russian, swarming with the Russians there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was one bakery there, whatever it was, a store. And my mother and I went in there. And two other women with us. Gertie was also going, but she kept going. We couldn't-- my mother-- my feet where there's her feet, where this. And so we were in this bakery. And there we got something to eat from the Russians. In fact, this was officer. Well, I don't if I tell this story. But after we were finished eating, he took me. And he took me into a room and throw me on a bed. And, of course, I knew what he wanted to do. And I start pushing him. And I ran outside. I ran outside and ran on the street. And I was crying-- crazy. And I told my mother we have to leave here. I can't stay here. And the women said, look at her! What she thinks they did to her. I mean, they couldn't believe it. I mean, the way I looked. And I was in the middle of the street, crying. And again, it was a miracle. A soldier came towards me. And he says to me in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him. I said, I was just released. We just came from concentration camp. And this and this happened. So he says-- he took me around. He says, my, my-- meine Schwester, my sister. Are you are alone? I said, no. I have my mother there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, get your mother. He was a Polish soldier who joined the Russian-- was in the Russian Army-- Jewish soldier. And he says, don't worry, I will protect you. Because these things were going on left and right, you know. The raping that was-- I didn't know that. But when I find it on my own, you know, then I found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took my mother and I. He brought us in one of the houses. And he gave us a room. I remember there was big pillows and big, you know, Like the down pillows, and things like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, you're going to sleep here. And I'm in the next room. I'm sleeping in the next room. He says, if anybody bothers you, you just knock, knock at the door. And I said, I will see that I can help you get to, to Vienna. And there he fed us with the pork they were making for the soldiers there, all kinds of, of, of mishmash there. But we ate it, you know-- anything we could. I mean, meanwhile, we had terrible diarrhea. And we ate this and ate this. We were-- we were terrible sick. But we had to eat. We felt we had to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one morning, he, he put us on a-- on a-- what do you call it? Again, a, a wagon-- not a car, a truck. It was a truck. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he put us on a truck with Russian soldiers, women soldiers. And they drove us to some kind of a station. And from there, we got a train to go to Vienna. And we arrived in Vienna early in the morning. And where the railroad station-- I know the street very well. We have been back since. Now it's a beautiful shopping street. But everything was bombed out-- bombed out. Everything was, like, flattened. And we walked, and we walked. And we set into the open store just to rest up. And I had a little bread and things in that satchel that I made in, in, in, in Mauthausen. And we walked all the way down. I remember we met somebody that we knew. And we ask him. He said, oh my god. [? Gutie, ?] you and Thea, you came back, you know. Where's [? Jule? ?] Well, he's not here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And George? We didn't know where my brother was. We didn't know that he survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went straight in a Jewish hospital on the Malzgasse. And there we stayed for weeks until we-- me, I recuperated much faster. And that was the end of our trip from, from Mauthausen. And we stayed in a hospital. I was out already. My mother was still in the hospital. And we had no place to live, no place to go. The-- there was nobody, actually that looked out-- the American Joint came a little bit later. So we had that maid that I mentioned that worked for us. She became a superintendent of a-- of a-- of a house. And she had a one-room apartment. And she took us in. My mother slept together with her on one bed. And I slept on an ironing board. That's how, how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, it got slowly better, you know. We got to eat. We recuperated. And then my relatives from Budapest, my grandparents, they also-- they went through their own horror. But they survived. And they told us to come, come for-- so we could recuperate. And so we went to Budapest, stayed there for a little while, got to eat, and went back to Vienna, then, afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you find out that your brother had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a long time later. My father-- we heard in, in Hungary. We-- they told us. They said, you know, [? Jule ?] did not survive. And George, we don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back to Vienna, I walked on the street. And I met a fellow that I knew from Theresienstadt. And I said, do you know anything about George?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, yes. George is alive. He's in a hospital in Germany. He has something-- he got very sick with his lungs. He was liberated in Auschwitz. And he's in a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it took us a while to, to get in touch with him. But finally, we got in touch with my brother. And he came to Vienna, like, in '46, '47. It took him a long time to recuperate. How about your mother? How did she fare after the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was pretty sick. She was sick in Vienna. And when we went to Budapest, my relatives there, they were all doctors-- my uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took her in the hospital. And she stayed there. Most of the time that we were in, in, in Budapest, she was in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very sick, undernourished. And, and, you know, and you're a little bit older. When you're married in your 40s, you, you couldn't take it as good as when you were in your teens.

speaker: interviewee
text: But finally, she recuperated pretty good that we said, we want to go back to Vienna, see if George, whoever, you know, we find. And don't stay there-- we're going to go either to Palestine or to, to America. Who would want to stay there? So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is that we couldn't come here again. We had to-- well, I met my husband, then, in Vienna. We were friendly with them. That's another story. But that becomes too long. We were living in the Russian Zone. Where the-- where the superintendent was, she was in the Russian Zone. And when you lived in the Russian Zone, you couldn't-- you couldn't come to America. I don't even know if I had an affidavit yet, but even to try.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so we stayed in Vienna. And I went back to school. At first-- at first, I took typing and shorthand so that I could make some kind of living. And-- because we had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was lucky. I got a [INAUDIBLE] with the American Joint Distribution Committee. And I worked in the office there. And they have-- they didn't get money. But we got food, food packages every week, which we could use and we sold, you know. On the black market we sold, whether we had sugar or margarine or spam, or something. And whatever we got for it, we bought for the house, for the-- oh, we got an apartment. My mother got us an apartment. Our old-- where we lived was completely bombed out. And, and that's where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Vienna until 1954. I came here. I went to school there. I did my-- I finished up. And I went two years to the university. And Gertie came here-- 1949. And so I came here for a visit in '54 to visit her. And that's when I met Jack again, my husband, whom I met in Vienna. He came back from, from the camp. And we were friendly again in Vienna. But he left already in '46, '47. And I went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- we met here again. And the whole flame started again. And we decided to get married here. May 28, 1955, we got married here. And so then you were able to stay in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a hard time staying here. First of all, I had to go back anyhow. I-- my mother couldn't-- still couldn't come here. But I arranged it. I went to Washington. I went to-- that time, was-- our senator was Javits-- intervened. And finally, they told me, the only one that can help her come here is the consul in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Vienna. And I had connections in Vienna. I knew the mayor of Vienna. And I went to him and asked him to write and, and vouch for my mother that she is not a communist or she is not a-- and so then my mother could come here, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she never immigrated here. She stayed in Vienna until she died. She came here a lot for a visit. But she never decided to stay, yeah. And this is the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a long story. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you and your husband have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we do have one daughter, Julia. And she has-- she's married. And she has three children-- Brian, Gregory, and Alex, 12, 9, and 6. And, and this is our life. She lives in Connecticut. And we are happy with them. We are happy with the grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is 'so far. And I hope it stays for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] Right. How, how do you feel that your experiences in the Holocaust affected your life after the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affected me a lot. First of all, you appreciated life much more, much more. And also, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] worthly goods, as nice as they are, they come and go. I mean, if you have a bigger car, a smaller car, a bigger house, a smaller house, and this, it never meant much to me. I'm not saying that I don't like it. But it doesn't mean anything to me. And my husband is the same way. Because we both went through the same situation, he even worse and longer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we never-- we discussed it. We knew about each other's life, what happened. But we didn't dwell on it because it's no good to dwell it now. When we get older, we feel we have to talk about it. People tell us we have to talk about it because once we are gone, there's denial already now. But in, in 50 years, there'll be much more denial. And that's why I find it so wonderful from Steven Spielberg that he, he manages and arranges all these interviews. Even though we didn't want to do it, we find now that it was our duty--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --duty to, to the people who got killed. Because there are many who don't believe it. They say, all of these pictures and all these stories are lies. And not 6 million were killed, only 1 million. It doesn't make any difference. But there were 6 million Jews killed. There were other people killed, too. But they decimated the Jewish-- half-- I think there were-- there were 12 before the war, 12 million Jews in the world. I think now may-- might be 14. And that that's-- so they decimated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK, we'll stop tape number 4 here and pick this up with tape number 5.

speaker: interviewee
text: No! [LAUGHTER]
